# **Spatial and Structural Analysis of the Label and Tag Printing Industry on Istanbul's European Side**

## **Taxonomic Discrepancies and NACE Classification Frameworks**

The classification of label and tag printing operates at the intersection of paper conversion, textile manufacturing, and commercial printing. Within the statistical system of the European Union and the corresponding registries of the Istanbul Chamber of Commerce (ITO), economic activities are categorized under specific NACE Rev. 2 codes.1 However, this structural categorization is undergoing a significant transformation due to the transition to NACE Revision 2.1, which mandates compliance across regulatory reporting, risk management, and ESG frameworks by a strict January 2026 deadline.3  
Label and tag manufacturers on Istanbul's European side primarily map to three core NACE classifications, each carrying distinct regulatory, administrative, and operational profiles:

* **NACE 17.29.01 – Manufacture of labels of paper or paperboard (*Kağıt veya mukavvadan etiketlerin imalatı*)**: This class encompasses self-adhesive, wet-glue, and dry paper labeling products.5  
* **NACE 13.96.04 – Manufacture of woven labels, badges, and similar items of textile materials (*Tekstil malzemelerinden dokuma etiket, rozet, arma ve diğer benzeri eşyaların imalatı*)**: This class covers woven brand identifiers, care labels, and embroidery emblems vital to the apparel industry.8  
* **NACE 18.12 – Other printing (*Diğer matbaacılık*)**: Often utilized as a broader umbrella category, this class encompasses generalized decal, sticker, hot-stamping, and screen-printing services that cross multiple material substrates.11

### **Classification Dynamics and Administrative Implications**

| NACE Code | Turkish Registry Description | Primary Substrates | Legacy ITO Professional Committee | OHS Hazard Classification | Active ITO Registry Count |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **17.29.01** | *Kağıt veya mukavvadan etiketlerin imalatı* | Paper, Paperboard, Self-adhesives | 6 – Paper, Stationery, and Packaging (*Kağıt, Kırtasiye ve Ambalaj*) | Hazardous (*Tehlikeli*) 6 | 0 5 |
| **13.96.04** | *Tekstil malzemelerinden dokuma etiket, rozet, arma ve diğer benzeri eşyaların imalatı* | Yarn, Synthetic Fibers, Ribbon | 42 – Textile Sub-Industry Products (*Tekstil Yan Sanayi Ürünleri*) | Less Hazardous (*Az Tehlikeli*) 8 | 719 8 |
| **18.12.01** | *Çıkartma, takvim, ticari katalog, poster vb. basım hizmetleri* | Vinyl, Plastics, Specialized Papers | 32 – Printing and Publishing (*Basım-Yayın*) | Hazardous (*Tehlikeli*) 14 | High (Distributed) 11 |
| **18.12.05** | *Serigrafi faaliyetleri* | Plastics, Metals, Glass, Textiles | 32 – Printing and Publishing (*Basım-Yayın*) | Hazardous (*Tehkelili*) 13 | High (Distributed) 11 |

A critical statistical anomaly exists within the official registries of the Istanbul Chamber of Commerce. Under the Paper, Stationery, and Packaging Professional Committee (No. 6), the active registry shows exactly zero companies registered under NACE 17.29.01 (*Kağıt veya mukavvadan etiketlerin imalatı*).5 In stark contrast, the Textile Sub-Industry Products Professional Committee (No. 42\) holds a robust cluster of 719 active firms mapped to textile label manufacturing under NACE 13.96.04.8  
This systemic discrepancy highlights a major taxonomic drift. Rather than registering under the dedicated paper conversion classification of 17.29.01 5, paper label printers almost universally register under the broader 18.12 (Other printing) category.11 This registration behavior is driven by two key factors:  
First, the printing of labels requires custom graphic replication, plate preparation, and ink-curing systems.17 These processes align operationally with generalized commercial printing houses rather than raw paper product conversion.16  
Second, occupational health and safety (OHS) regulations impose different compliance burdens based on these classifications. Under Turkish labor laws, both NACE 17.29.01 and 18.12 are designated as "Hazardous" (*Tehlikeli*), requiring intensive safety assessments and more hours of certified OHS personnel per worker.7 Textile label weaving under NACE 13.96.04 is classified as "Less Hazardous" (*Az Tehlikeli*).10 This regulatory gap creates financial and legal incentives for multi-substrate label manufacturers to register under textile-related codes. Doing so allows them to minimize administrative overhead while continuing to print on various materials.8

### **Regulatory Pressures of the NACE Rev. 2.1 Transition**

This historical misclassification poses significant operational risks as the January 2026 deadline for NACE Rev. 2.1 implementation approaches.4 The European Central Bank and the European Banking Authority have mandated that all prudential, supervisory, and resolution reporting—including monthly AnaCredit data points at the granular 4-digit level—must use the updated taxonomy.4  
Regulatory bodies are enforcing a zero-tolerance policy for misclassified economic codes.4 As a result, commercial printing and packaging firms in Istanbul face sudden banking and supply chain compliance checks.4 Local manufacturers must now establish dual-code tracking databases to map legacy classifications to the updated system.4 This alignment is necessary to prevent credit restrictions, export delivery delays, or compliance failures when trading with European corporate clients.4

## **Registry of Active Printing and Labeling Enterprises on the European Side**

A cross-referenced analysis of active label printing and manufacturing entities registered with the Istanbul Chamber of Commerce (ITO) reveals a robust ecosystem of specialized firms.2 The following table profiles active enterprises on Istanbul’s European side that specialize in paper, textile, and industrial label production.

| Company Name | Registered District | Operational Address | Verified Contact Details | Primary Product Focus & Substrates |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Dizayn Etiket Sanayi Ticaret A.Ş.** | Bahçelievler (Yenibosna) | Çobançeşme Mah. Sanayi Cad. | **Bengisu Etiket (Etiket Matbaa ve Tekstil Aksesuarları)** | Zeytinburnu | Maltepe Gümüşsuyu Caddesi Odin Topkapı Merkez 28/192, D: A Blok, 34010 Zeytinburnu, İstanbul 37 | Phone: \+90 212 612 05 25 37 | Apparel trim accessory printing, fabric tags, and specialized brand accessories 37 |

## **Spatial Optimization and Micro-Geographic Clustering**

The label and tag printing industry on Istanbul’s European side is organized into distinct geographic clusters.22 These hubs are structured around proximity to primary manufacturing sectors, supply chain corridors, and real estate configurations.31

### **Spatial Dynamics of Key Clusters**

| Cluster Region | Primary Districts Included | Key Industrial Complexes | Dominant Substrates & Technologies | Spatial & Logistics Characteristics |
| :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- | :---- |
| **Legacy Zeytinburnu-Topkapı** | Zeytinburnu, Topkapı, Bayrampaşa | Kale İş Merkezi, Emintaş Sanayi Sitesi, Litros 2\. Matbaacılar Sitesi 44 | Paper, Board, Foils; Sheet-fed offset, screen printing 44 | High spatial density, multi-tenant vertical blocks, immediate access to paper and plates 30 |
| **Merter Apparel Trim Axis** | Güngören, Merter, Nesih Özmen | Keresteciler Sitesi, Savaş Caddesi, Aytek Plaza 23 | Woven yarns, cotton ribbons, synthetic fabric tags 8 | Close proximity to fashion houses, design-driven rapid turnarounds 23 |
| **Industrialized Başakşehir** | Başakşehir, İkitelli | İkitelli OSB, ESKOOP, İPKAS, İsteks 39 | Vinyl, PP, PET, complex paper composites; Flexography, rotary press 39 | Large horizontal factory floors, heavy power access, integration with chemical suppliers 40 |
| **Peripheral Esenyurt-Arnavutköy** | Esenyurt, Arnavutköy, Deliklikaya | Teskoop Sanayi Sitesi, Osmangazi Industrial Zone 27 | Multi-layered adhesive films, packaging boards; High-speed digital conversion, UV offset 17 | Modern standalone facilities, proximity to major highway networks, international compliance 27 |

### **The Legacy Zeytinburnu-Topkapı Cluster**

The Zeytinburnu and Topkapı districts represent the historic, high-density core of Istanbul’s commercial printing sector.44 This cluster relies on close physical proximity to auxiliary services.44 Multi-tenant complexes, such as the *Kale İş Merkezi* on Davutpaşa Avenue and the *Emintaş Sanayi Sitesi*, house numerous micro-workshops.35  
These firms share access to specialized paper merchants, plate manufacturers, ink distributors, and machine mechanics located within the same industrial blocks.30 This shared network allows local firms, like ASL Etiket and Alan Etiket, to reduce turnaround times and transaction costs.30 These operations can complete and deliver urgent, short-run packaging orders within hours to clients across the city.30

### **The Merter Apparel Trim Axis**

Adjacent to the legacy print hub, Güngören’s Merter district serves as the primary design and sourcing center for the Turkish fashion export market.23 The labeling firms clustered here—such as Ares Etiket and CR Etiket—focus on garment and textile accessories.22  
These operations rely on close integration with local fashion design houses and textile dye networks.23 Because apparel branding relies on fabric-soft materials, Merter's label production lines are optimized for yarn-weaving looms (Jacquard machines) and rotary screen-printing.8 This setup allows firms to rapidly produce woven brand labels, satin care labels, and paperboard hangtags that meet the aesthetic and quality requirements of global clothing brands.8

### **The Industrialized Başakşehir Core**

As environmental regulations, municipal zoning rules, and space constraints limited central urban workshops, larger label manufacturers migrated west to İkitelli Organized Industrial Zone (*İkitelli OSB*) in Başakşehir.33 İkitelli OSB provides heavy-load floors, stable electrical grids, and dedicated waste management infrastructure.40  
This setup allows firms like Yeniler Etiket, Etimat Etiket, and ITL İstanbul Etiket to install large, modern machinery, including multi-color flexographic printers and automated rotary conversion lines.33 This industrial cluster primarily serves high-volume packaging sectors, such as pharmaceuticals, cosmetics, and automated logistics, where label consistency and chemical resistance are critical.39

### **The Peripheral Esenyurt and Arnavutköy Frontier**

The westernmost edge of Istanbul's European side represents the newest phase of industrial relocation.27 Esenyurt and Arnavutköy (specifically the Deliklikaya industrial area) host large, standalone manufacturing facilities.27  
Firms like Sps Etiket and Olcay Etiket operate from these areas.27 This allows them to avoid the space limitations of urban clusters and design their production layouts for optimal material flow.28  
Positioned along major transport corridors (the O-7 and E-80 highways), these facilities are well-integrated into regional and international logistics networks. They are optimized to process large volume orders of roll-fed self-adhesive labels and industrial decals for global export, operating in compliance with international environmental standards.12

## **Global Supply Chain Integration, Materials Science, and Trade Compliance**

Label and tag manufacturers on Istanbul’s European side are critical integrators within international supply networks.49 Rather than acting as isolated print shops, these firms serve as specialized compliance and branding partners for international consumer brands.17

### **Subcontracting Networks and Brand Protection**

Under the guidance of initiatives like the Istanbul Subcontract Exchange—established with the support of the United Nations Industrial Development Organization (UNIDO)—local label manufacturers are integrated into the global supply chains of major European and North American retail networks.49 Because labels are the primary vector for product authentication, variable shipping data, and regulatory compliance (including fiber composition, origin labeling, and safety symbols), these printers occupy a high-liability position in the manufacturing pipeline.  
Advanced firms, such as Dizayn Etiket, manage international operations (including subsidiaries in global trade centers like Hong Kong) to maintain seamless variable data integration across decentralized manufacturing networks.21 This integration is critical for retail brands that require synchronized label printing across multiple regional sewing or assembly sites.

### **Technical Transformations in Production**

The printing and label conversion processes have shifted from basic mechanical layout printing to complex material engineering.17 The production profiles of modern firms show a transition toward advanced, multi-substrate technologies:  
![][image1]  
This technological progression requires significant capital investment. For example, local operations must support UV offset, rotary hot-stamping, and digital printing on a single line to produce anti-counterfeiting labels, integrated RFID smart tags, and sustainable paperboard packaging.17  
Additionally, the transition to circular economy mandates in Western Europe forces local manufacturers to source eco-certified raw materials. This includes FSC-certified paper backings, water-soluble adhesives, and recycled polyester (rPET) yarns.12 Label manufacturers must ensure that their products do not compromise the recyclability of the primary packaging, making adhesive selection a critical technical competency.

## **Strategic Outlook and Recommendations**

The label and tag printing industry on Istanbul's European side is a highly developed industrial ecosystem characterized by distinct geographic zoning, advanced technical capabilities, and integration into global supply networks.40  
The sector's spatial distribution reflects its functional alignment. The legacy urban clusters of Zeytinburnu and Güngören maintain their role as flexible, rapid-turnaround partners for the apparel accessory market.23 Meanwhile, the modern industrial parks of Başakşehir, Esenyurt, and Arnavutköy support high-volume, automated production lines for logistics, chemical packaging, and export industries.27  
The upcoming enforcement of NACE Rev. 2.1 will require administrative adjustments from these firms.4 To prevent regulatory friction, companies must carefully align their registration profiles with their actual manufacturing activities. This alignment is essential for accurate ESG reporting, risk rating, and compliance auditing in export markets.4  
Furthermore, as global demand shifts toward smart, sustainable packaging solutions, the long-term competitiveness of Istanbul's label printing sector will depend on continuous capital investment. To maintain their position in international markets, firms must focus on adopting digital print technologies, incorporating recycled materials, and ensuring compliance with evolving international standards.12
